content.problemStatement:
You are given a **binary** string SSS of length 2⋅N2\cdot N2⋅N.

A partition of string SSS into strings PPP and QQQ is said to be valid if:


Both PPP and QQQ are subsequences of SSS of length NNN;
Each character of string SSS belongs to **exactly one** subsequence out of PPP and QQQ;
Strings PPP and QQQ are **NOT** equal.
Let AAA denote the set of indices of SSS that belong to the subsequence PPP.

Find whether it is possible to make a valid partition of SSS.

If it is possible, print the elements of array AAA, in increasing order. Otherwise, print −1-1−1.


Input Format

Each test contains multiple test cases. The first line contains the number of test cases TTT. Description of the test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN, where 2⋅N2\cdot N2⋅N is the length of the binary string.
The second line of each test case contains the binary string SSS of length 2⋅N2\cdot N2⋅N.


Output Format
For each test case, if a valid partition exists, print NNN distinct indices A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ (in **increasing** order).

If no valid partition exists, print −1-1−1.

If there are multiple solutions, print any.
constraints: It is guaranteed that the sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 10510^5105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: It can be proven that no valid partition exists.
Test case 222: A valid partition is P=1P = 1P=1 and Q=0Q = 0Q=0. Here, A=[1]A = [1]A=[1].
Test case 333: A valid partition is P=100P = 100P=100 and Q=000Q = 000Q=000. Here, A=[1,2,3]A = [1, 2, 3]A=[1,2,3].
Test case 444: A valid partition is P=110P = 110P=110 and Q=000Q = 000Q=000. Here, A=[1,4,6]A = [1, 4, 6]A=[1,4,6].
